content.text: Write solutions in a neat and logical fashion, giving complete reasons for all steps. Attempt SIX problems.

problem.text: For any two of the following, either give an example or explain why no example exists.

subparts.label: a)
subparts.text: a sequence of Lebesgue measurable functions on \(\mathbb R\) which converges in measure but not Lebesgue a.e.

subparts.label: b)
subparts.text: a closed set \(E\subset[0,1]\) with positive Lebesgue measure, but which contains no open intervals

subparts.label: c)
subparts.text: a decreasing sequence of nonnegative measurable functions on \(\mathbb R\) such that \(\lim \int f_n \ne \int \lim f_n\)

problem.text: State Tonelli’s theorem, and give an example to show that the \(\sigma\)-finiteness hypothesis is necessary.

subparts.label: b)
subparts.text: State and prove a version of the chain rule for Radon–Nikodym derivatives.

problem.text: Suppose that \(f:\mathbb R\to\mathbb C\) is a Lebesgue measurable function with the property that \(fg\in L^1(\mathbb R)\) for every \(g\in L^2(\mathbb R)\). Must \(f\) belong to \(L^2(\mathbb R)\)? Prove, or give a counterexample.

subparts.label: a)
subparts.text: Show that for every integer \(n\ge 1\), there exists a function \(f_n\in L^2[0,1]\) such that for every polynomial \(p\) of degree at most \(n\), \[p(0)=\int_0^1 p(x)f_n(x)\,dx.\]

subparts.label: b)
subparts.text: Prove that there is no \(L^2\) function \(f\) such that the above holds for all polynomials \(p\) (independent of the degree \(n\)).

problem.text: State the Baire category theorem. Prove that if \(\mathcal X\) is an infinite-dimensional Banach space, then \(\mathcal X\) does not have a countable basis.

Let \(\mathcal X\) be a Banach space and \(\mathcal Y\) a normed vector space. Let \((T_n)\) be a sequence of bounded linear operators from \(\mathcal X\) to \(\mathcal Y\). Suppose that \(\lim_n T_nx\) exists for each \(x\in\mathcal X\). Prove that the linear transformation \(Tx:=\lim_n T_nx\) is bounded from \(\mathcal X\) to \(\mathcal Y\).
For each of the following statements, determine if it is true or false, and sketch a proof of your claim.

subparts.label: a)
subparts.text:
If \(
u\) is a signed measure and \(E,F\) are measurable sets with \(
u(E)\ge 0\) and \(
u(F)\ge 0\), then \(
u(E\cup F)\ge 0\).

subparts.label: b)
subparts.text: If \((X,\mathcal M)\) and \((Y,\mathcal N)\) are measurable spaces and \(E\subset X\), \(F\subset Y\) are sets such that \(E\times F\) is \(\mathcal M\otimes\mathcal N\)-measurable, then \(E\in\mathcal M\) and \(F\in\mathcal N\).
